sentences.text: We went-- [LAUGHS] we were walking.
Went [INAUDIBLE].
Everybody who-- who could go was very lucky, so we went.
We went, and we were walking.
As I said, it wasn't too long.
I think it was about three miles, four miles, something like this-- maybe five miles.
Not too much.
This was not the worst.
No.
Nothing.
You went like to work!
The liquidation was the way that every group was standing, every working group was standing together.
And they-- they controlled if this is really-- everybody has the identity card, everybody belongs to the-- to the-- to the specific group which has to go out, let's say, to-- to place x or y or z.
And then, if-- if they accepted it, they go out!
So, instead to going to work, we went-- we were directed to Plaszow.
So, like we went to the work!
We went there.
And in Plaszow we got special clothes, so we didn't need anything.
This were civil clothes, not the stripes.
It was civil clothes, painted.
'Painted,' it means there were some stripes made with-- with colored, in order to make us look special.
And we cannot run away in such a-- so, it was a cross through the-- through the pants, and then the blouse you got.
I think we got also-- also a sweater, I think.
A few things.
All the-- the whole camp was built from barracks.
And there were two-- two-floor beds.
There was some three-floor beds.
And everybody had his place, his barrack.
Mostly the barracks were-- the whole working group got one barrack or two barracks.
And there was a big kitchen, which divided the daily food for everybody.
That means people also-- the-- the working groups were coming together, at the special hour, and were getting the soup.
And every morning, we got a portion of bread, a little bit of margarine, and jam, a little bit of jam.
And this was the portion for the whole day.
And at noon, we got so-called soup, which was-- at the beginning, it was-- something was in.
Even some meat, mostly potatoes or other things.
With the time, it was more water than-- than soup.
But this was the hot-- so-called hot meal once a day.
But, again, most of the time, when we have been in Plaszow, there were many, many groups going-- continuing to go out to work, like from ghetto.
To the same places.
So, again, the contact-- contacting the Poles, and the possibility of at least eating outside.
I myself was working the same garage, in the beginning.
And then, then it stopped.
Then it stopped.
I don't remember if the garage was liquidated, or we had been thrown out.
Something happened, and then I began to work in the-- most of us stopped-- At the end, nobody went out.
With the time, the, the camp was closed.
With the time, the working camp changed to be concentration camp, also.
So, many were working-- most of us were-- not 'almost,' with the time, everybody was working inside.
Because they stopped allowing people to, to, to be in touch with-- with the-- with the world.
Part-- part-time, yes, and part-time not.
There was a--
--time that we lived in the same barrack, and then was time that not.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah--
--you could, you could.
You have even contact with women.
Not physically.
You couldn't stay with them, but you can meet them, during the day, and so on.
There was a special part for women, in Plaszow.
You couldn't go to stay the night there or in the evenings.
Evenings, a little bit, but not too much.
But you could meet.
This was not the problem.
The very well known Amnon Goeth 'Amon'-- Amon Goth, who was hanged.
He was on trial in Poland, in Krakow, after the war.
He was caught in Austria and was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] taken to Poland, or given to Poland, and was on trial, was hanged in, I think, in '46.
I don't remember the date.
I wasn't in Poland at that time, so I--
I was working in so-called bathroom and dis-- disinfections rooms-- appliances of-- from-- of the camp.
It was the place where people got their baths, and the clothes were-- went under disinfection.
Which means, the Germans were very afraid of typhoid, of black typhoid.
And that was-- this was something that they were afraid more than to go-- to go into the army and to the front.
So they paid a big attention to it, that the people should go through the so-called disinfection.
And they called it 'Entlausung.' I don't remember exactly how often.
Once, two-- two months, or something like this.
So, every inmate had to go through the bathroom, to give up his clothes.
They were put in a, in a, in a big oven, let's say.
I don't know how to call it.
And there was heat of such a heat that should-- I don't know if it did, but should-- kill the, the germs and all-- all possible insects, which were causes of typhoid, specially.
And, on the other side, in the meantime, they got the baths and the showers.
And, after 45 minutes, they got their clothes back, and it was-- it was called 'disinfected.' And so I-- I was working there.

Again, I was lucky enough to work there, because, first of all, it was a very hard work, because, to, to, to put these things into this ovens, and to take them out, when they were very, very hot, it was a very hard work.
But-- and, again, I'm jumping-- by the way, I was one of those who got fleck-- or fleck fever or black fever, fleck fever from, from one of the small animals-- which I got there.
But it was a good place to work, because we weren't freezing.
We could sleep over, there.
We weren't obliged to go to-- because we were working also nights.
The baths was working 24 hours.
And it was powered by, by, by coal-- uh-- uh-- no.
Coal!
So, there was fire, so we could-- we could boil whatever we-- if we had something.
So, this was called a very, very good working place.
Not to go to barracks!
You-- you have to stay in barrack with 200, 300 people in one place.
And here were a few of us.
And this is something.
So we had-- we had a place where we settle our mattresses.
We slept on [INAUDIBLE].
And we stayed there.
It was warm, nice, and clean.
So this was very, very good.
And it was also a very dangerous-- the other side was also dangerous, but, because through the same bathroo-- bath, uh--
--bathhouse were coming the, the watchmans-- the Ukrainians, the Latvians, Lithuanians, were the guards of the camp.
And this was something to, to, to serve them.
We have to serve-- we had to serve them.
And you had to control the, the, the water, to make it warm enough or cold enough or how they want it.
And they want it-- they want it it's always otherwise than you did it.
And by the time they didn't get what you wanted, they beat you.
So, it was-- But, in general, it was a very-- it was a good place, in-- if you-- the alternatives were much, much worse.
Yeah, I was infected, and I got black fever, or fleck fever.
And I was taken to the hospital.

But, after a while, the doctors knew that if the Germans will know that I am infected by, by fleck fever, I have to be shot immediately, because they were so afraid of epidemic that they were always looking after it.
Owing to my cousin, who was one of the doctors of the-- I was kept there as somebody who has-- I don't remember what.
Something internal.
And they didn't know.
But I was unconscious for 10 days.
But came through.
I went-- sure.
Now, then, you are-- you can work already without any fear.
You can't get twice the same.
So.
I don't know if I am-- I was afraid.
You know, you have to know that with the time, we-- we got only used to be in danger every day, every minute, every hour, every night.
We were-- it's a hard word to say we got used to it, but we are not surprised if, in the morning, all of the camp had to stand-- Oh, by the way, all of the camp had to stand on so-called appellplatz.
It means on the place where people were gathering before going to the work.
And they counted everybody.
Or they didn't count every-- Every man who was responsible for his group had to come out and to say, my-- this and this group, so many people are present.
And so many people are sick in the barrack, and so many people are in the hospital.
So we, from the bathhouse, we didn't go to the appellplatz.
We were allowed not to take part.
So this was, again--
So, standing in this appellplatz, you never know, knew, if it ends only by counting and then released and go to the work, or there was some aktia, or some-- at that time, it was only selections.
It was not called 'aktia,' it was selection.
Because mostly, then, at that time, they selected weak people, elder people, sick people, people who didn't look strong enough.
If they decided--
Again, having, from the very beginning, knowing that they need today, so, they sent this amount of people to be sent away.
So, they took away the elderly, the very young.
And, with the time, it's very well known, from Plaszow, the story.
At the beginning, there were many children in Plaszow, many, many children, which are grouped in a special barracks.
And there came a day when all the children were taken away, with a lot of music, with all the camp standing on the-- on the-- on the place, and children going away.
And this was something that I won't forget.
So, with the time, we, we-- I call it, we-- we became stoned.
We stopped feels anything.
We-- we stopped being afraid.
We stopped hoping.
We stopped-- we stop arguing.
We stopped awaiting.
And what I find, or found, or find, after their war, the worst.
They succeeded in making, in changing us from human to animal.
Because, at least myself, but I spoke afterwards with many, many friends of mine.
So, many of them have same reaction, the same feelings.

If there was a selection, if something happened, and people were sent to death, or people was shot, or people were selected taken away, and we knew they won't come back, or we saw how they shot them-- never mind how-- what-- in what way they-- they killed them, the first feeling, or the first reaction wasn't, oh, how I am sorry.
Even if-- if it was a-- somebody whom I was related with, or a very good friend of mine, or somebody who stood next to me or slept next to me.
So the first feeling wasn't, oh, how sorry I am, how terrible it is he went.
The first feeling was, how good it is I was left.
And this was the big-- the victory of the Germans, in my opinion.
Any of your family was part of a selection, during your time in Plaszow?
As I told you, in Plaszow nobody was left.
All of us which were left-- ah!
I forgot.
Once, my mother, my-- my-- ah, it was very dramatic.
You see, I forget.
Once there was a very big selection.
Thousands were sent away.
And among them was my mother and my sister.
And even at that moment, we saw them go away, from-- from very far, I don't remember that I was very, very moved.
I don't remember that I was crying.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember, or I remembered, that I was glad this was after the selection of the men.
You know?
It didn't go together.
So the women were brought away.
And the-- the train which took away the people was-- they had to march, half a mile, not more.
There was such a special station arranged.
So, you could even see from the very far.
So the women were taken away.
And if I'm not-- yeah, no, I am not mistaken.
We are dispersed already.
We-- we could go-- we could move from the place.
This-- such a-- such a selection, it was a whole day!
So we have to stand there, at least all the whole day, three quarters of a day.
And then there came the order to barracks.
So, we kept walking to the barrack.
And, once we saw a small group of-- of women, coming back to the camp.
And my mother and my sister were among these women.
Now it's again the story which I told you before, from ghetto and from--
They had an amount of people whom they wanted to send away.
And they packed them all in the-- in the-- into the wagon-- into the--
--trains.
The cattle-- cattle wagons.
And there was no-- no place even to pack them.
There's a limit how-- even if-- if it goes-- all these were sent to-- to death.
All these-- so it didn't mean too much to-- to keep them only standing.
But even to stand, they have to-- to put only a limited amount of people.
So it came out that, I think, 200 or 300 were left.
And these two were left, among them.
And-- and they succeeded to survive, afterwards.
Now, at that time, the joy was a very big one.
But I still insist on the-- that the feeling of losing somebody wasn't so-- so dramatic as it should be that time.
And, as I said, I think this was the big-- the biggest victory of-- to-- to-- to transfer the human to-- to animals.
Yeah.
It is the last part of my--
This is last part of my career as a-- as a-- as a war inmate.
When Plaszow had to be liquidated, we-- all the time before, we knew who is Schindler.
We knew what he's doing for the Jews.
We knew what an influence Schindler has on the commanding officer of Plaszow.
We knew how much he could help in-- in keeping Plaszow, because there was a time when they wanted to liquidate Plaszow before and to send away all the people to death.
And, owing to Schindler, or partly owing to Schindler, it was omitted.
Everybody knew that if there is a paradise during this time, it is the place where Schindler has his factory.
And there was a group of some 300, 300, 350 people, working and living around Schindler.
He had a factory in Krakow, and he succeeded-- He was very influential.
He-- he was-- he made a lot of money during the war.
He got the factory from Jews.
He sold at least half of the-- of what they made there on black market.
He made a lot of money.
And he knew how to bribe the right people, in the right places, in the right-- in the right time.

So, he succeeded, because of all these tricks, to-- that he got the permission to settle a small camp around his factory, and to keep these 350 people around him, being affiliated, or something like this, to Plaszow.
That means, it was a part of Plaszow.
But they, at that time, already, it was very well known that they are living like in paradise.
You should know, it was all the-- always relatively to-- to something.
So, at the time when we knew already, and everybody knew that the time comes that Plaszow will be liquidated, we knew that we, in-- in best case, will be sent to Germany, to other camps.
And these were mostly camps where people, if they were not determinated physically, immediately, sent to ovens, to gas chambers [INAUDIBLE].
So they were determinated by-- in a very short time-- by very hard work.
Don't forget, it was already the last year of the war.
So, then we heard that Schindler decide-- not 'decide'-- decided, and got the permission, to-- Ah!
In the moment when-- when Plaszow is liquidated, also his place will be liquidated, because his camp is-- is a part of Plaszow.
So he decided to, to, to use another trick.
And he got the permission from capital of Germany, from Berlin, to move his factory from Krakow through Germany.
But in this time, he won't make any--
--pots and pans, but he will make out of it something ammunition.
That means, more important for the-- for the army and for the-- for the war effort.
It was-- everything was called-- you have to, to, to contribute to the war effort of-- of Germany.
This was the slogan.
So he bribed-- again, he bribed where he had to bribe, and the right people, in the right places, in this right moment.
And he got the permission to move his factory or to build a new factory in Sudeten, in today's Czechoslovakia-- uh, Czech.
But at that time it was German.
And, to go there.
So--
